risk_identified: Uncapped FFS may lead to providers charging high co-payments, excluding high-need/low-income populations.
structural_mitigation_proposed: Mandatory co-payment caps or zero-fee tiers for high-need groups as a condition of accessing the scheduled FFS stream.

risk_identified: New supply (clinics/appointments) clusters in affluent urban areas rather than high-deprivation or rural areas.
structural_mitigation_proposed: Place-based accountability contracts; higher scheduled FFS rates (rural/deprivation loading) for targeted areas.

risk_identified: Expanded supply may be culturally unsafe, deterring Māori and Pacific whānau.
structural_mitigation_proposed: Conditioning FFS eligibility on cultural safety accreditation and integration with Kaupapa Māori providers.

risk_identified: Data generated by new claims is not governed by or visible to Māori, hindering responsive policy.
structural_mitigation_proposed: Te Tiriti-led data governance over the NPCD and claims data; shared monitoring of access targets.

risk_identified: FFS incentives prioritize quick, simple issues (cream-skimming) over complex, multimorbid care.
structural_mitigation_proposed: Strong capitation base retained for continuity; FFS item rules that adequately compensate extended/complex consultations.
